context:
## Dieses Dokument

- Titel: Radio Address to the American
                                Schoolchildren
- Signatur: SZ-AP2/W-H522b.2
- Datum: April 1939
- Sprache: Englisch
- Objekttyp: Typoskriptdurchschlag
- Umfang: 5 Blatt,
                                                  korrigiert
- Hand: Lotte Zweig
- Anmerkungen: Korrekturen von Lotte Zweigs Hand

pages.transcription:
Radio Address to the
American Schoolchildren
(short wave to Cincinnati, Apr. 1939)

Dear young Friends,

In this moment I am speaking to you from
another continent. I speak from London, in Europe.
If you think that you've just had breakfast, while
we have finished our lunch long ago, and are already
looking forward to our dinner, it will give you an
idea of how far away it is from your home. It takes
about one week for a steamer to cross the ocean that
lies between you and me, an aeroplane takes two days,
and even the good old sun shines on you six hours
later than it does on us. Maybe you aren't
surprised at all {these modern marvels,} this, you are quite used to having
the radio in the room, and think nothing of humanity
having such good ears, and such long ones, that {hundreds of millions} we
are able to hear each word or melody at the same
time, over every country and across all seas. As
for me, I cannot help but wonder at it afresh every
time I think of it. I remember the time when all
these wonderful inventions, which are {nowadays} part of your
daily life, were not yet {invented.} (For example I
remember quite clearly how in my native town of
{in Austria}
Vienna my parents had the telephone installed. All
the neighbours who'd never seen one before, kept
running in to convince themselves with their own ears,
that this small black shell really did transmit voices
twenty streets away, and the tube carried one's own
voice back in answer. Our old cook, however, who
came from a small village, could never be induced to
touch this diabolical machine, and every time the
bell rang, she ran and hid.
pages.notes: Typoskript mit handschriftlichen Korrekturen und Ergänzungen (vermutlich Lotte Zweig). Die Einfügungen sind über den Zeilen platziert.

pages.transcription:
- 2 -

After all what I have told you, you might
imagine that I am at least a hundred years old. An
old man with a snow white beard. However, I must
remind you that it is only about fifty years ago
that we in Europe first had the telephone laid on
in the towns, that it is only forty years since one
started to drive a car properly, and only thirty
years since aeroplanes really can fly. Just think,
it is only twelve years ago since Lindbergh flew
across the ocean to Europe. All these technical
marvels which seem quite natural to you, haven't
existed for very long. The other day, while I was
still amongst you in America, I spoke in Detroit to
an old man who had helped your great Edison to invent
the electric light, who was present at the historical
moment when the light flashed up for the first time
in a little glass bulb - the same electrical light
without which you can't imagine either your houses,
theatres, cinemas or streets. Time just flies, my
dear young friends, as you'll soon find our for your-
selves, and never has the human mind produced so many
new and astonishing things as in our century. That
is why you must'nt think that you may stop learning
the day you leave school. Human progress does'nt
stop where our schoolbooks do, and we must get used
to the idea that life teaches us new things till the
day we die. That is just the beautiful and the
wonderful part of it, that one's experiences never
come to an end. Being an author by profession,
perhaps I'm just the right person to assure you of
all this.

- 3 -

Now - perhaps you will ask: why should a
writer know more about this than other people?
An author, my dear young friends, is a man
whose job it is to describe. To describe things that
happened years ago, or even things which never
happened at all, except in his imagination. And to
be able to describe well, one must first of all
observe well. Nothing in this world, however small
it is, is therefore unimportant to him. To interest
one's readers, it is necessary to be interested one-
self in what one relates to them. I think the only
reason why I became an author was, that ever since I
can remember, my ambition was to understand every-
thing. If there was something I didn't understand,
I never rested till I had got to the bottom of it.
That is the only way how you can explain it to others.
I still remember exactly how I started to
write. It was like this: I always had a passion of
reading, and as I said before, I was most inquisitive
as a child. I wondered how such interesting books
came to be written. But alas, as a boy there was
no writer I knew personally, and I had no one to ask
how to write poems or stories. So I just took my
chance - I must have been about fourteen in those
days - and I started to write.
I found it easy at first. I just sat down
and wrote the story as I would a school essay, only
with more pleasure, because I was so proud of writing
something no one else had ever written before, and
which hadn't been imposed by a teacher. But alas,
my joy did'nt last long. When I showed the story to
my friends they weren't a bit enthusiastic. They said

- 4 -

it was dull and not well written. But this first
failure did'nt discourage me. I wrote a second story
and tried to write it better, and it was whilst trying
again and again, that I gradually realised how much
more I had to learn to become a real author. It
became clear to me that in this, as in every other
profession, it takes years and years of patient
training till one has the right to be satisfied with
one's work.
Many years have gone by. Since then I have
written many books, and I've had the happiness to know
that hundreds of thousands, yes, even millions of
people have read these books in every conceivable
language. But I stand just where I was forty years
ago, that is to say, every time I try that my new
work should be an improvement on the last one in
order not to disappoint my readers. I go on trying
to widen my knowledge, to learn more, always more.
The thought that I should say one day: "Now I know
enough" or "Now I have learned enough" is dreadful
to me. I want to go on learning and to write many
more books, better books and of ever varying subjects,
and if I envy you a little, my young friends who are
just starting life, it is only because of all the
surprising experiences which are in store for you.
How many new and wonderful discoveries and
inventions will you live to see, how many marvels
which we do not even dream of now. How much richer
and more varied your life will be, you who are growing
up in a free country, in a wonderful century! And so
my heartiest wish to you is that you should always

- 5 -

consciously enjoy all that comes to you, and that
you should have a heart full of gratefulness to
be able to enjoy it all.

And so from London good-bye.

result.confidence_notes: Der Text ist sehr gut lesbar, die handschriftlichen Korrekturen sind eindeutig zuzuordnen.
